title: Nobody gets an A: Future of Life Institute's Summer 2026 Safety Index grades nine AI labs
subtitle: Anthropic tops the list with a C+, OpenAI and Google DeepMind each pull a C, and xAI, DeepSeek, and Mistral fail outright — the report documents a broad industry retreat from safety pledges since 2024.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: The Future of Life Institute published its Summer 2026 AI Safety Index on July 7, grading nine leading AI companies across 37 indicators spanning six domains: risk assessment, current harms, safety frameworks, existential safety, governance and accountability, and information disclosure. No company earned an A or B. Anthropic topped the ranking with a C+. OpenAI and Google DeepMind each received a C. xAI, China's DeepSeek, and France's Mistral each received an F.

The report's more consequential finding is not the letter grades but the trend line. Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and Meta all previously pledged to halt or restrict development if their models approached specific capability thresholds — Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy, OpenAI's Preparedness Framework, and their peers' equivalents. FLI documents that those pledges have been quietly weakened or abandoned since 2024. In February 2026, Anthropic withdrew its commitment not to train systems unless it could guarantee its safety measures were sufficient in advance. All four companies also reversed prior bans on military applications between 2024 and 2026, joining xAI and Mistral in actively pursuing defense contracts.

The index lands in the same week the UN Independent Scientific Panel on AI, co-chaired by Yoshua Bengio and Maria Ressa, told the Geneva Global Dialogue that no technical guarantee of AI safety currently exists and that capabilities are advancing faster than any government's ability to regulate them. Together they mark the industry-observer consensus solidifying around one uncomfortable conclusion: self-regulation is no longer functioning as a check, and no external one has replaced it. Grades of C+ and below across the entire frontier are the signal, not the noise.

For learners: notice how the story of the last two years reversed. In 2023 and 2024 the leading labs competed on safety commitments. In 2025 and 2026 they competed on capability and quietly dropped the commitments. That trajectory is not inevitable — it is a choice made by companies, boards, and buyers. When you pick which models to build with, which subscriptions to fund, and which employers to work for, you are voting on which trajectory continues. Read the safety pages, ask the hard questions in interviews, and treat the answers as data.

sources.title: AI Safety Index — Summer 2026 — Future of Life Institute
